text:

Mourn, Empress of the hushed and shadowy night!  
And ye, bright starnies, glittering in your flight,  
For Matthew mourn!  
Through all your orbs he’s borne his soul from sight,  
Ne’er to return.
source_text:
Mourn him, thou Sun, great source of light!
Mourn, Empress of the silent night!
And you, ye twinkling starnies bright,
My Matthew mourn!
For through your orbs he’s ta’en his flight,
Ne’er to return.

text:
"Love! thou art guiding me from winter's chill,
Lady! thou lead'st me unto summer's air,
And I must drink the blossoms, opening still
Within its mellow warmth this gracious morn of prayer."
Thus spake he, and his once-timid lips grew brave,
And mingled verse with hers in dewy rhyme:
Great bliss abode with them, and great happiness
Rose, like a lusty flower in June's caress.
source_text:
"Love! thou art leading me from wintry cold,
Lady! thou leadest me to summer clime,
And I must taste the blossoms that unfold
In its ripe warmth this gracious morning time."
So said, his erewhile timid lips grew bold,
And poesied with hers in dewy rhyme:
Great bliss was with them, and great happiness
Grew, like a lusty flower in June's caress.

text:
They were two cousins, near as twins to view,  
Save that from out the calendar of sins  
Nature had blotted their affection—torn  
Asunder only by their births forlorn.  
And so together they upgrew, like flowers  
That on one stem drink one sun, one rain of showers,  
That in their purple season either lull  
Or wake, the same hand gathering when full;  
The same fair clime shall wither them in death.  
This lovely day smiles upon all that breathe  
In love—and who loved ever as didst thou,  
Fiordispina? Hardly Cosimo, now  
Whose heart and brain are kindled through and through  
With such a vision as eclipses view  
Of that fair idol from whose form it came.  
He faints, dissolved in a great sea of flame;  
But thou art like a planet, throned above;  
But thou art very Love, that rules the move  
Of his enthralled soul: such passion must  
Find sin and sorrow, had not gentle August May  
Brought forth this morn—your wedding-day.
source_text:
They were two cousins, almost like to twins,
Except that from the catalogue of sins
Nature had rased their love—which could not be
But by dissevering their nativity.
And so they grew together like two flowers
Upon one stem, which the same beams and showers
Lull or awaken in their purple prime,
Which the same hand will gather—the same clime
Shake with decay. This fair day smiles to see
All those who love—and who e’er loved like thee,
Fiordispina? Scarcely Cosimo,
Within whose bosom and whose brain now glow
The ardours of a vision which obscure
The very idol of its portraiture.
He faints, dissolved into a sea of love;
But thou art as a planet sphered above;
But thou art Love itself—ruling the motion
Of his subjected spirit: such emotion
Must end in sin and sorrow, if sweet May
Had not brought forth this morn—your wedding-day.

text:
MAMMON:
Disinherited
My first-born son Chrysaor, for he
Went to the public gatherings, and would ever
Stand there babbling of trade, of public trust,
Of thrift, and pure unmixed coin,
And other matters, ultra-radical;
And would have settled my estate, named the Fool’s Paradise,
And sums in fairy-money, bonds, and bills,
Upon my trained daughter Banknotina,
And wed her to the gallows.

text:
Though feeble as the lama that springs on the mountains,
And gifted not with the swift-winged footfalls of air,
Yet, yet shall I drink from the purest of fountains,
Though a fiercer than tiger is lurking there.
Though, more dreadful than death, it doth scatter despair,
Though its shadow doth darken the day,
And the gloom of the deepest dismay
Diffuses the power of soul-chilling terror around,
And broods o'er the corpses that moulder on the ground.

Though weak as the lama that bounds on the mountains,
And endued not with fast-fleeting footsteps of air,
Yet, yet will I draw from the purest of fountains,
Though a fiercer than tiger is there.
Though, more dreadful than death, it scatters despair,
Though its shadow eclipses the day,
And the darkness of deepest dismay
Spreads the influence of soul-chilling terror around,
And lowers on the corpses, that rot on the ground.

text:
From Freedom’s shape divine,
From Nature’s inmost shrine,
Strip every impious idol, rend
Delusion veil by veil;
O’er Ruin, desolate,
O’er Falsehood’s fallen state,
Sit thou sublime, unawed; let the Destroyer pale!
And equal laws be thine,
And wingèd words let sail,
Freighted with truth e’en from the throne of God:
That wealth, surviving fate,
Be thine.—All hail!
source_text:
From Freedom’s form divine,
From Nature’s inmost shrine,
Strip every impious gawd, rend
Error veil by veil;
O’er Ruin desolate,
O’er Falsehood’s fallen state,
Sit thou sublime, unawed; be the Destroyer pale!
And equal laws be thine,
And winged words let sail,
Freighted with truth even from the throne of God:
That wealth, surviving fate,
Be thine.—All hail!

text:
I call no Goddess forth to fire my strains,  
A feigned Muse may suit the bard who feigns:  
Friend of my life! my fervent spirit burns,  
And all the tribute of my heart returns,  
For boons bestowed, for goodness ever new,  
The gifts yet dearer, as they came from you.  
Thou orb of day! thou other, paler light!  
And all ye myriad sparkling stars of night!  
If aught that giver from my mind efface,  
If I that giver’s bounty e’er disgrace,  
Then roll to me along your wand’ring spheres,  
Only to number out a villain’s years!  
I lay my hand upon my swelling breast,  
And grateful would, but cannot speak the rest.
source_text:
I call no Goddess to inspire my strains,
A fabled Muse may suit a bard that feigns:
Friend of my life! my ardent spirit burns,
And all the tribute of my heart returns,
For boons accorded, goodness ever new,
The gifts still dearer, as the giver you.
Thou orb of day! thou other paler light!
And all ye many sparkling stars of night!
If aught that giver from my mind efface,
If I that giver’s bounty e’er disgrace,
Then roll to me along your wand’rig spheres,
Only to number out a villain’s years!
I lay my hand upon my swelling breast,
And grateful would, but cannot speak the rest.

text:
There lay Lorenzo, slain and laid within,
There in that forest ended his great love;
Ah! when a soul doth thus its freedom win,
It aches in loneness, ill at peace thereof
As the break-covert blood-hounds of such sin:
They dipped their swords in water, and did move
Their horses homeward, with convulsed spur,
Each wealthier by his being a murderer.
source_text:

It aches in loneliness--is ill at peace

They dipp'd their swords in the water, and did tease

text:
SEMICHORUS 2:
There the wanton nightingales
Are waking through the broadest noon.
When one in bliss or sorrow fails,
And through the windless ivy-boughs,
Sick with sweet love, droops faint and dies
Upon its mate’s music-panting breast;
Another from the swaying nest,
Watching the languid ending close
Of the last strain, then lifts on high
The wings of the feeble melody,
Till some new thrill of feeling bear
The song, and all the woods grow mute;
Then is heard through the shadowy air
The beat of wings, and mounting there
Like many a flute by waters set,
Sounds overflow the listener’s brain
So sweet, that joy is nearly pain.
source_text:
SEMICHORUS 2:
There the voluptuous nightingales,
Are awake through all the broad noonday.
When one with bliss or sadness fails,
And through the windless ivy-boughs,
Sick with sweet love, droops dying away
On its mate’s music-panting bosom;
Another from the swinging blossom,
Watching to catch the languid close
Of the last strain, then lifts on high
The wings of the weak melody,
Till some new strain of feeling bear
The song, and all the woods are mute;
When there is heard through the dim air
The rush of wings, and rising there
Like many a lake-surrounded flute,
Sounds overflow the listener’s brain
So sweet, that joy is almost pain.

text:
I honor Assyria, China, Teutonia, and the Hebrews,  
I take to me each doctrine, myth, god, and demigod,  
I discern that the ancient chronicles, bibles, genealogies, are true, without  
exception,  
I avow that all bygone days were what they needs must have been,  
And that they in no wise could have been better than they were,  
And that to-day is what it must be, and that America is,  
And that to-day and America in no wise could be better than they are.
source_text:
I respect Assyria, China, Teutonia, and the Hebrews,
I adopt each theory, myth, god, and demigod,
I see that the old accounts, bibles, genealogies, are true, without
exception,
I assert that all past days were what they must have been,
And that they could no-how have been better than they were,
And that to-day is what it must be, and that America is,
And that to-day and America could no-how be better than they are.

text:
On the fairest time of June
Thou mayst go, with sun or moon,
Or the seven stars to light thee,
Or the polar ray to right thee;
Yet thou never shalt behold
Little John, nor Robin bold;
Never one, of all the clan,
Thrumming on an empty can
Some old hunting ditty, while
He doth his green way beguile
To fair hostess Merriment,
Down beside the pasture Trent;
For he left the merry tale
Messenger for spicy ale.

text:
Hark to me, ye reverend core,
As counsel for poor mortals
Who oft do pass through Wisdom’s door
For Folly’s foolish portals:
I, for their heedless, careless sake,
Would here propose defences—
Their droll misdeeds, their dark mistakes,
Their failings and mischance.
source_text:
Hear me, ye venerable core,
As counsel for poor mortals
That frequent pass douce Wisdom’s door
For glaikit Folly’s portals:
I, for their thoughtless, careless sakes,
Would here propone defences—
Their donsie tricks, their black mistakes,
Their failings and mischances.

text:

text:
And aged Silenus, wavering a green staff  
Of lilies, and the wood-gods in a band  
Came, merry, as in the olive thickets thick  
The cicadae come, with noonday dew made drunk:  
And Dryope and Faunus came apace,  
Baiting the God to sing them somewhat new;  
Till in this cave they found the lady lone,  
Sitting upon a seat of emerald stone.
source_text:
And old Silenus, shaking a green stick
Of lilies, and the wood-gods in a crew
Came, blithe, as in the olive copses thick
Cicadae are, drunk with the noonday dew:
And Dryope and Faunus followed quick,
Teasing the God to sing them something new;
Till in this cave they found the lady lone,
Sitting upon a seat of emerald stone.

text:

Till the lifeless air takes soul
With the clatter of the wheels,
And the thunder of the steeds’ heels.
source_text:
‘Let the charged artillery drive
Till the dead air seems alive
With the clash of clanging wheels,
And the tramp of horses’ heels.

text:

Also, who guides the gentle spheres
By withes of pliant blue?

source_text:
Also, who laid the rainbow's piers,
Also, who leads the docile spheres
By withes of supple blue?

Who counts the wampum of the night,

text:

May still on thy Chloris fall, Love!
O wert thou, &c.
source_text:
But, dreary tho’ the moments fleet,
O let me think we yet shall meet;
That only ray of solace sweet,
Can on thy Chloris shine, Love!
O wert thou, &c.

text:
I shall make a song for these States, that no single State may, under any  
conditions whatsoever, be brought beneath another State,  
And I shall make a song that there be comity by day and by  
night among all the States, and betwixt any two of them,  
And I shall make a song for the President’s hearing, full of  
weapons with threatening points,  
And behind the weapons innumerable discontented faces;  
And a song make I of the One wrought out of all,  
The fang’d and glittering One whose head is over all,  
Resolute warlike One encompassing and above all,  
(Howe’er high the head of any other, that head is over all.)
source_text:
I will make a song for these States that no one State may under any
circumstances be subjected to another State,
And I will make a song that there shall be comity by day and by
night between all the States, and between any two of them,
And I will make a song for the ears of the President, full of
weapons with menacing points,
And behind the weapons countless dissatisfied faces;
And a song make I of the One form’d out of all,
The fang’d and glittering One whose head is over all,
Resolute warlike One including and over all,
(However high the head of any else that head is over all.)

text:
“An ye prattle here the livelong day, justice shall go astray.  
Think ye the culprits will bide within their father’s house?  
They are in flight, they wait not.  
See that a band of horse be sent upon their trail.”
source_text: “An ye prattle here all the day, justice will miscarry. Think ye the criminals will abide in their father's house? They are fleeing, they are not waiting. You should look to it that a party of horse be set upon their track.”

text:
The men, flung out in party-factions,
Then settle a’ in dark debauches.
Ae night they’re daft wi’ drink and whoring,
The neist day life is past enduring.
source_text:

Then sowther a’ in deep debauches.
Ae night they’re mad wi’ drink an’ whoring,
Niest day their life is past enduring.

text:
Many things I teach, to aid thee in becoming eleve of mine;  
Yet if blood like mine course not in thy veins,  
If thou be not in silence chosen by lovers, nor in silence choose lovers,  
Of what avail is it that thou seekest to become eleve of mine?
source_text:

Yet if blood like mine circle not in your veins,
If you be not silently selected by lovers and do not silently select lovers,

text:

Converse of the "early dead,"
We, prone to circumlocution,
Observe that birds are fled!
source_text:

Chat of the 'early dead,'
We, prone to periphrasis,
Remark that birds have fled!

text:
‘Time passed—I know not whether months or years;
For day, nor night, nor turn of seasons made
Its mark, but thoughts and tears that nothing cheers:
And I grew, in the end, as one half-shade,
A smoke, a cloud the winds have worn and frayed,
Till it was thin as air; until, one eve,
A Nautilus upon the fountain strayed,
Unfurling his blue sail where Heaven’s breath did not leave,
Among the waves and whirling eddies driven.
source_text:
‘Time passed, I know not whether months or years;
For day, nor night, nor change of seasons made
Its note, but thoughts and unavailing tears:
And I became at last even as a shade,
A smoke, a cloud on which the winds have preyed,
Till it be thin as air; until, one even,
A Nautilus upon the fountain played,
Spreading his azure sail where breath of Heaven
Descended not, among the waves and whirlpools driven.

Some close, foul, dungeon'd cave,

source_text:
What dost thou in that mansion fair?
Flit, Galloway, and find
Some narrow, dirty, dungeon cave,
The picture of thy mind.

text:
Thus—though uncreative—
A clear, intense apprehension
Of his mind’s labor had made alive
The things on which it wrought; I deem
It waked a kind of thought in sense.
source_text:
Thus—though unimaginative—
An apprehension clear, intense,
Of his mind’s work, had made alive
The things it wrought on; I believe
Wakening a sort of thought in sense.

text:
Just now I’ve ta’en the fit o’ rhyme,
My daftish noddle’s in full prime.
My fancy’s sprung up high and fine,
At sudden call;
Hae ye a little leisure-time
To hear the whole?
source_text:
Just now I’ve ta’en the fit o’ rhyme,
My barmie noddle’s working prime.
My fancy yerkit up sublime,
Wi’ hasty summon;
Hae ye a leisure-moment’s time
To hear what’s comin?
